title: The Macro Minute: Is the US economy too resilient?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here at Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Thursday,
September 25th, 2025. As always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. If you
would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk, need to be a client of 42
macro. So today's key macro question is,
is the US economy too resilient? Our
answer, no. Despite the positive
revision of Q2 GDP, downside surprises
in initial and continuing jobless
claims, and upside surprises in August
durable goods and former capital goods
new orders, each supportive of our
resilient US economy theme. We do not
view the US economy as currently being
too resilient for the current FOMC to
satisfy the latest dot plot projection
of two more rate cuts between now and
year end. The weak positive impulse to
an above trend growth rate of 3.8% 8% uh
in headline real GDP and the weak
positive impulse to a trend growth rate
of 2.8% 8% in real final sales masked
weakness under the surface. The US
economy actually contracted at a well
below trend growth rate of minus 1.9%
after netting out government and net
exports. The strong negative impulse of
real GDPX government and net exports is
at this level of growth is concerning.
The weak positive impulse in non-farm
productivity growth and weak negative
impulse in unit labor cost inflation are
supportive of corporate profitability
for now. How long can investors expect
this favorable dynamic to persist if
nine out of every 10 jobs being created
is in a government or government
adjacent sector? The current 90% share
of non-farm payrolls growth is outsized
relative to the total stock of
government and government adjacent jobs
which are currently only 32% of the
total. Moreover, government and
government adjacent payrolls are
currently growing three times as fast as
private sector services payrolls and 240
basis points faster than the minus 1%
contraction in private sector
manufacturing payrolls. And then uh I
didn't write this down, but um in terms
of the 90% share of non-farm payrolls
growth uh uh in terms of nine out of
every 10 jobs, that's since the start of
the second Trump administration. And
that um roughly 90% share is up from
about 70 75% over the final two years of
the Biden administration. So, we're
literally going in the wrong direction
in terms of uh quote unquote draining
the swamp. So, all told, uh we have long
maintained the low point of the U-shaped
economy is likely to be Q4 of this year
and expect to see a continued slowing of
high and low frequency growth indicators
in the month ahead. in the months ahead.
Uh this likely outcome will continue to
weigh on hiring activity in the labor
market which supports further
riskmanagement rate cuts by the Fed, an
institution we believe is in the very
early days of undergoing structural
regime change that is likely to produce
a far more dovish policy setting 12 to
18 months from now. Current market
pricing of 1.5 cuts through year end is
reasonable. Transitioning to our 42
micro dashboard as always wrap up with a
question from our community. Uh this
one's uh not quite a question but a good
comment. Uh so we'll just read it. says,
"Uh, George Gammon interview, it's
titled George Gam interview with DD."
Says, "Good morning, 42 micro community.
Yesterday, I had the chance to watch
this interview between George Gam and
Skipper. This is one of the more
insightful videos I've watched recently,
and I recommend checking it out. The
reason I got so much out of it was that
George reminds me of a typical
intelligent investor. I follow George,
but he actually reminds me of myself. He
has a good understanding of macro. He
looks into headlines, and he thinks
critically, but he doesn't have a
research edge over the layman. His
economic outlook mirrors many prominent
macro bears, focusing on the plethora of
data trending negatively, which I tend
to do as well. Skipper gave an
explanation of the U-shaped economy
thesis that made it truly land for me,
and it was fun to see George press and
challenge DD, which prompted DD to break
things down in more detail than usual.
It was fun being the fly on the wall for
two friends talking macro. So, uh,
thanks for the the kind feedback and and
certainly ditto to all that. Um, you
know, George is a really really bright
guy and I had a really awesome time
getting to know him uh and and and
befriending him uh at Hugh Henry's asset
capitalist summer camp um last month. Um
you know obviously a very bright guy and
has a real sort of thoughtful and
critical kind of framework by which he's
applying uh uh looking to you know
macroeconomic risk and ultimately market
risk. You know we disagreed on quite a
quite a lot of stuff. U I think our core
kind of fundament underlying thought
processes are relatively um you know not
relatively similar. we arrived at a lot
of the same conclusions despite
disagreeing on the the path we took uh
to get there. Uh and so I thought that
was a really good interview. You know,
it's not quite uh an institutional level
climate meeting, but certainly was along
that d in that general direction in
terms of, you know, push back, you know,
point counter point counter point, you
know, push back here and there. So, I
thought it was a to this point to the
point of the the comment, it was a
really good um uh piece of content in
terms of, you know, watching, you know,
two smart people go at it in terms of um
in terms of trying to figure out the
right answer uh for the audience. So, uh
to the extent you guys have not reviewed
that, I highly recommend you review that
um that that interview with uh with
George. I think it was published on
Monday or Tuesday of this week. So,
we'll wrap it up there. Derry St here

luck out there today. Hey, we'll catch
